reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Formal Languages

map.condition: Prove a language is NOT regular
Check if regular languages are closed under an operation
Identify language class from grammar production rules
Find minimum pumping length for a given regular language
map.use: p \ge \text{number of states in minimal DFA for } L
\text{index of } \equiv_L = \text{number of states in minimal DFA}

formulas.name: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages
formulas.latex: \forall L \in \text{RL},\; \exists p \ge 1: \forall w \in L,\; |w| \ge p \Rightarrow w = xyz \text{ where}\\|xy| \le p,\; |y| \ge 1,\; \forall i \ge 0: xy^iz \in L
formulas.plain: For every regular L, there exists pumping length p >= 1 such that every w in L with |w| >= p can be split into w = xyz where |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1, and xy^i z in L for all i >= 0.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a language is NOT regular by contradiction — assume L is regular, choose a long string, show no valid split can be pumped.

terms.means: pumping length (>= number of states in minimal DFA)
the chosen string of length >= p

formulas.trap: Pumping lemma is NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT for regularity — a non-regular language can satisfy the pumping lemma; the lemma only lets you DISPROVE regularity.

formulas.name: Minimum Pumping Length
formulas.latex: p_{\min} = \text{number of states in minimal DFA for } L
formulas.plain: p_min = number of states in the minimal DFA for L
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the smallest valid pumping length when given a concrete regular language defined by a DFA or regular expression.
formulas.trap: Any p >= p_min is also a valid pumping length; the question asks for 'a' pumping length, not necessarily the minimum.

formulas.name: Pumping Lemma for CFLs
formulas.latex: \forall L \in \text{CFL},\; \exists p: \forall w \in L,\; |w| \ge p \Rightarrow w = uvxyz \text{ where}\\|vxy| \le p,\; |vy| \ge 1,\; \forall i \ge 0: uv^ixy^iz \in L
formulas.plain: CFL pumping lemma: w = uvxyz, |vxy| <= p, |vy| >= 1, uv^i xy^i z in L for all i >= 0.
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing CFL from CSL/recursive; also used in questions asking what can be concluded if L satisfies BOTH pumping lemmas.

terms.means: the two pumpable substrings (at least one non-empty)

formulas.trap: Satisfying both the regular AND CFL pumping lemma does NOT make L regular or even context-free — L could be any language.

formulas.name: Closure Properties of Regular Languages
formulas.plain: Regular languages are closed under: union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, reversal, homomorphism, inverse homomorphism.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions asking which operations preserve regularity — ALL listed operations keep the result regular.

terms.means: complement of L (all strings NOT in L)

formulas.trap: Regular languages ARE closed under intersection (unlike CFLs) — a common trap question pairs RL closure with CFL closure.

formulas.name: Closure Properties of CFLs
formulas.latex: \text{CFL closed under: } \cup,\; \cdot,\; {}^*,\; h(L),\; h^{-1}(L),\; L^R\\\text{CFL NOT closed under: } \cap,\; \overline{L}
formulas.plain: CFL closed under union, concat, Kleene star, homomorphism, inverse hom, reversal. NOT closed under intersection and complement.
formulas.whenToUse: Compare with RL closure — the key contrast is that CFL is NOT closed under intersection or complement, but RL IS.
formulas.trap: CFL intersection with a REGULAR language IS closed (CFL ∩ RL = CFL); only CFL ∩ CFL is not guaranteed to be CFL.

formulas.name: Closure Properties of Recursive and RE Languages
formulas.latex: \text{Recursive: closed under } \cup, \cap, \cdot, {}^*, \overline{L}\\\text{RE: closed under } \cup, \cap, \cdot, {}^* \text{ but NOT } \overline{L}
formulas.plain: Recursive languages are closed under all Boolean operations including complement. RE languages are NOT closed under complement.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about which language class is closed under complement — only RL and Recursive are; RE and CFL are not.
formulas.trap: The complement of an RE language need not be RE; if both L and its complement are RE, then L is recursive.

formulas.name: Intersection: RL ∩ CFL = CFL
formulas.plain: Intersection of a regular language and a CFL is always a CFL.
formulas.whenToUse: Reducing a CFL problem using a regular mask/filter; proving a language is CFL by intersecting with a RL.
formulas.trap: CFL ∩ CFL is NOT necessarily CFL, but RL ∩ CFL IS always CFL — this distinction appears often in GATE.

formulas.name: Chomsky Hierarchy (containment)
formulas.plain: Regular subset Context-Free subset Context-Sensitive subset Recursive subset Recursively Enumerable.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which class a given language belongs to; establishing that every regular language is also CFL, CSL, etc.
formulas.trap: Inclusions are STRICT — for each level there exist languages in the outer class but not the inner class.

formulas.name: Chomsky Grammar Types
formulas.latex: \text{Type 3 (RL): } A \to aB \text{ or } A \to a\\\text{Type 2 (CFL): } A \to \alpha,\; \alpha \in (V \cup T)^+\\\text{Type 1 (CSL): } |\alpha| \le |\beta| \text{ for } \alpha \to \beta\\\text{Type 0 (RE): no restriction}
formulas.plain: Type 3: right/left-linear rules. Type 2: any single nonterminal on LHS. Type 1: non-contracting (|lhs| <= |rhs|). Type 0: unrestricted.
formulas.whenToUse: Given grammar production rules, classify the grammar type by checking production form constraints.
formulas.trap: A grammar with BOTH left-linear and right-linear rules (mixed) is NOT a regular grammar — it may generate a non-regular CFL.

formulas.name: Right-Linear Grammar (Type 3)
formulas.latex: A \to aB \;|\; a \;|\; \varepsilon \quad (A, B \in V,\; a \in T)
formulas.plain: Right-linear: every production is A -> aB or A -> a or A -> epsilon.
formulas.whenToUse: Converting an NFA to a regular grammar; or checking that a given grammar generates a regular language.
formulas.trap: Left-linear grammars ALSO generate exactly the regular languages, but mixing left-linear and right-linear rules in a single grammar can go beyond regular.

formulas.name: Myhill-Nerode Theorem
formulas.latex: L \text{ is regular} \Leftrightarrow \text{index of } \equiv_L \text{ is finite}\\\text{index of } \equiv_L = \text{number of states in minimal DFA}
formulas.plain: L is regular iff the number of Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes (strings indistinguishable by L) is finite. The number of classes equals the state count of the minimal DFA.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a language is non-regular (infinite classes), or finding the number of states in the minimal DFA.

terms.means: x ~_L y iff for all z: xz in L <=> yz in L

formulas.trap: Myhill-Nerode gives NECESSARY AND SUFFICIENT conditions (unlike pumping lemma which is only necessary), so it can definitively prove regularity or non-regularity.

formulas.name: Arden's Lemma
formulas.latex: X = AX + B \Rightarrow X = A^*B \quad (\text{if } \varepsilon \notin A)
formulas.plain: If X = AX + B (and epsilon not in A), then X = A*B. Used to solve systems of regular equations from state diagrams.
formulas.whenToUse: Converting a DFA/NFA state equations into a regular expression by solving the linear system.
formulas.trap: If epsilon IS in A, the solution is not unique — Arden's lemma applies only when epsilon is not in A.

formulas.name: Classic Non-Regular Languages
formulas.latex: \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\},\; \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \ge 0\},\; \{ww^R \mid w \in \Sigma^*\},\; \{0^p \mid p \text{ prime}\}
formulas.plain: {a^n b^n}, {a^n b^n c^n}, {ww^R (palindromes)}, {0^p | p prime} are all non-regular.
formulas.whenToUse: Quick lookup when identifying whether a given language is regular or not based on structural patterns.

terms.means: n a's followed by n b's (requires counting)

formulas.trap: {0^p | p prime} is non-regular but satisfies the pumping lemma — only Myhill-Nerode or direct argument can prove non-regularity here.

formulas.name: Classic Regular Languages
formulas.plain: {a^n}, {a^(2n)}, strings whose length is divisible by k, all strings over Sigma — these are regular.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying that fixed-modulus counting patterns are regular (can be encoded in a finite DFA).

terms.means: set of all strings over alphabet Sigma

formulas.trap: {0^n | n is composite} is regular (complement of {0^p | p prime} union {0, epsilon}); primality itself is the non-regular constraint.

formulas.name: Programming Language Classification
formulas.plain: Programming languages like FORTRAN, C, Pascal are context-free (syntax described by CFG). Individual tokens (identifiers, numbers) are regular.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions asking which class a real programming language (FORTRAN, C) belongs to — always context-free at the syntactic level.
formulas.trap: FORTRAN is NOT regular (it has matching structures like begin-end blocks) even though its tokens are regular — lexical and syntactic levels differ.

formulas.name: Read-Only TM = DFA (Regular Languages)
formulas.latex: \text{TM with read-only input tape and O(1) work tape} \equiv \text{DFA} \Rightarrow \text{recognizes exactly RL}
formulas.plain: A Turing machine with a read-only input tape and constant-size (O(1)) work tape recognizes exactly the class of regular languages.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions relating restricted Turing machine models to simpler automata classes.
formulas.trap: With O(log n) work tape a TM recognizes exactly the context-free languages; O(1) specifically gives regular.

formulas.name: Reversal Preserves Regularity
formulas.plain: If L is regular then the reversal language L^R is also regular.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving the reverse of a regular language is regular; or applying closure under reversal to construct a new regular language.

terms.means: language of all reversals of strings in L

formulas.trap: Palindrome language {ww^R} is NOT regular even though reversal is a closure operation — the closure applies to the LANGUAGE L^R, not to the set of palindromes.

formulas.name: Kleene's Theorem (RL Equivalences)
formulas.plain: DFA, NFA, epsilon-NFA, regular expressions, and regular grammars all describe exactly the same class of languages (regular languages).
formulas.whenToUse: Justifying conversions between representations; any of the five models can be used interchangeably to describe a regular language.
formulas.trap: NFA does NOT recognize more languages than DFA — they are equivalent in power despite NFA allowing nondeterminism.

formulas.name: NFA to DFA (Subset Construction) State Bound
formulas.plain: Converting an NFA with n states to a DFA may require up to 2^n states (subset construction).
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating the size of the equivalent DFA; worst-case analysis of NFA-to-DFA conversion.

terms.means: number of states in the NFA
maximum number of DFA states after subset construction

formulas.trap: The worst case 2^n is achievable; but many practical NFAs convert to DFAs with far fewer states.
